buggy_code:
import pandas as pd

# Multi-asset price DataFrame indexed by date
prices = pd.DataFrame({
    'AAPL': [100, None, 102, None, 104],
    'MSFT': [200, 201, None, None, 205]
}, index=pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=5))

# Reindex to full date range and forward fill ALL columns together
prices = prices.reindex(pd.date_range('2020-01-01', '2020-01-05')).ffill()

# Compute returns on filled data
returns = prices.pct_change()

# Use as features
features = returns.dropna()
clean_code:
import pandas as pd

prices = pd.DataFrame({
    'AAPL': [100, None, 102, None, 104],
    'MSFT': [200, 201, None, None, 205]
}, index=pd.date_range('2020-01-01', periods=5))

# Forward fill EACH column independently with a limit
# so gaps longer than 1 day are NOT filled
prices = prices.ffill(limit=1)

# Flag remaining NaN as genuinely missing — don't trade those days
valid_mask = prices.notna().all(axis=1)
prices = prices[valid_mask]

# Compute returns only on valid data
returns = prices.pct_change().dropna()
description: prices.reindex().ffill() forward fills ALL missing values across ALL assets with no limit.
explanation: When a stock has missing prices (e.g. halted trading, data gap), ffill() silently propagates the last known price forward indefinitely. This means: (1) pct_change() returns 0% on filled days, making the stock appear to have no volatility — a false signal. (2) If one asset has a 3-day gap and another has none, their returns are computed on mismatched real trading days. (3) In a multi-asset portfolio, this creates fake correlations — filled assets appear to have returns of exactly 0% while others have real returns.
suggested_fix: Use ffill(limit=1) to fill only single-day gaps. For longer gaps, drop the rows or mark them as invalid. Never compute returns or signals on forward-filled prices without validating the fill length.

issues.title: Unbounded forward fill on multi-asset prices distorts returns
